title: Kenya, France Sign 11-Point Deal Covering Logistics, Rail, and Energy
excerpt: Kenya and France have signed 11 bilateral agreements spanning port logistics, commuter rail, agriculture, digital infrastructure, and energy deals set to reshape Kenya's supply chain landscape.
author: Staff Writer
body:
Kenya and France have signed 11 bilateral agreements spanning port logistics, commuter rail, agriculture, digital infrastructure, and energy deals that are set to reshape Kenya's supply chain landscape in the coming years.

The signing took place on May 10, 2026, at State House Nairobi, during a meeting between President William Ruto and French President Emmanuel Macron, who was in the country for the Africa Forward Summit, the first edition held outside France or a Francophone nation in over 50 years.

The most significant deal for the logistics sector is a joint venture between the Kenyan government and shipping giant CMA CGM to develop port and logistics infrastructure, valued at approximately KSh 104 billion.

The partnership is expected to strengthen Mombasa's role as a key trade gateway for East and Central Africa.

On rail, Kenya and France agreed to a KSh 12.5 billion rehabilitation of the Nairobi Commuter Rail network, extending corridors to satellite towns including Syokimau, Embakasi, Ruiru, and Kikuyu, a move that could ease last-mile distribution pressures across the capital.

Other agreements cover the export of Kenyan specialty teas into French retail markets, a KSh 32.5 billion expansion of the Kipeto Wind Energy project, sustainable aviation fuel production, nuclear energy cooperation, and digital infrastructure development.

President Ruto summed up Kenya's foreign policy position simply: "We are not looking East or West. We are looking forward."

title: KEMSA Strengthens Healthcare Supply Chain Ties with China
excerpt: Kenya Medical Supplies Authority hosted a high-level Chinese delegation for strategic discussions focused on transforming the country's healthcare supply chain.
author: Staff Writer
body:
Kenya Medical Supplies Authority (KEMSA) recently hosted a high-level Chinese delegation for strategic discussions focused on transforming the country's healthcare supply chain, a significant step towards enhancing Kenya's healthcare infrastructure.

The high-profile engagement brought together KEMSA leadership and Chinese experts to explore collaborative opportunities aimed at modernizing and strengthening the systems responsible for delivering essential medicines and medical commodities across Kenya.

During the visit, the delegation was given an in-depth tour of KEMSA's operations, gaining firsthand insight into the end-to-end processes of procurement, warehousing, inventory management, and nationwide distribution of health products to public health facilities.

Discussions centered on key areas of mutual interest, including supply chain digitization, warehousing optimization, distribution efficiency, and innovative approaches to inventory control systems.

Both parties expressed strong commitment to leveraging technology, improving logistics, and enhancing accountability to ensure more reliable and efficient service delivery in the health sector.

KEMSA used the occasion to reaffirm its dedication to continuous improvement, with the goal of guaranteeing timely access to quality, affordable health products and technologies for all Kenyans.

title: BPP Backs NDDC's Procurement Digitization for Enhanced Transparency
excerpt: The Bureau of Public Procurement has endorsed the Niger Delta Development Commission's procurement policy reform and digitisation initiative as a step toward greater transparency.
author: Staff Writer
body:
The Director-General of the Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP), Dr. Adebowale Adedokun, has endorsed the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) procurement policy reform and digitisation initiative, describing it as a major step toward improving transparency, efficiency, and service delivery in public governance.

Speaking at the NDDC Procurement Policy Reform and Digitisation Stakeholders Sensitisation and Training Summit in Port Harcourt, Adedokun said the transition to paperless governance had become essential as government institutions seek to modernise operations, reduce delays, eliminate waste, and improve accountability in public procurement processes.

According to him, procurement remains central to government service delivery because it determines how public resources are allocated and how development projects reach citizens.

He stressed the importance of collaboration among government agencies to ensure a seamless transition to digital systems, noting that the reform aligns with President Bola Tinubu's broader agenda of building a more responsive and citizen-focused public sector.

In his remarks, Dr. Samuel Ogbuku said the workshop was designed to strengthen institutional capacity and align the Commission's operations with global best practices.

He stated that the Niger Delta Development Commission (NDDC) was moving from "transaction to transformation" by embracing digital procurement systems that enhance accessibility, reduce corruption, and improve transparency.

Ogbuku added that all contractors doing business with the Commission would henceforth engage through an online procurement platform designed to improve workflow and accountability.

title: BPP, NADDC Sign Nigeria First Policy
excerpt: The Bureau of Public Procurement and the National Automotive Design and Development Council have signed off on full implementation of the Nigeria First Policy in the automotive sector.
author: Staff Writer
body:
Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP) and the National Automotive Design and Development Council (NADDC) signed off on the full implementation of the Nigeria First Policy in the automotive sector by all procuring entities.

The signing of the policy now makes it an offence for government agencies to procure vehicles from any source other than local manufacturers, including vehicle assembly plants operating in Nigeria.

The Director-General of the BPP, Dr. Adebowale Adedokun, noted that the initiative would further empower Nigeria and Nigerians.

The DG said: "Today marks an important step in strengthening the integrity, accountability and developmental impact of public procurement in Nigeria. With the implementation of this policy on the Nigeria First Policy in the Automotive Sector, public procurement is being more deliberately aligned with national economic priorities and with the Federal Government's commitment to support local production through public expenditure.

"For us at BPP, this is about ensuring value for money, transparency, lawful process and measurable national benefit. Public funds used for vehicle procurement must not only procure assets for government use, but must also support industrial growth, reduce procurement abuse and improve accountability across the system."

With this, the next step will be the issuance of a Revised Circular by the appropriate government authority.

On his part, the Director General/CEO of NADDC, Otunba Oluwemimo Osanipin, who commended the DG, BPP, for being proactive and for fast-tracking the implementation process, said: "Today, under the guidance of the Nigeria First Policy, we are fundamentally shifting that paradigm and the implementation of this policy is not merely about procurement; it is an economic imperative."

title: Guinea Digitizes Public Procurement With Launch of TELEMO Platform
excerpt: Guinea has launched TELEMO, a national digital procurement platform developed with Rwanda, aimed at transforming how government contracts are managed and awarded.
author: Staff Writer
body:
Guinea has taken a major step toward modernizing its public sector operations with the launch of TELEMO, a national digital procurement platform aimed at transforming how government contracts are managed and awarded.

Introduced in Conakry, the platform marks a shift away from paper-based procurement systems toward a fully digital framework designed to improve efficiency, transparency, and oversight across public contracting processes.

The initiative was developed in collaboration with Rwanda, a country widely recognized for its advances in digital governance and public service digitization.

TELEMO centralizes the full procurement cycle within a single online platform, allowing institutions to handle procurement planning, tender announcements, bid submissions, evaluations, contract awards, and document archiving electronically.

Authorities expect the system to significantly reduce administrative delays while improving accountability in public spending.

The rollout forms part of Guinea's broader efforts to modernize public financial management and accelerate the digital transformation of government services.

Public procurement plays a crucial role in the national economy, representing between 11% and 15% of Guinea's GDP, making the sector a strategic area for reform.

The government also sees the platform as a way to improve access to public contracts for small and medium-sized enterprises by simplifying participation and increasing visibility into procurement opportunities.

Both local and international companies will be able to access procurement processes through the online system.

Across West Africa, public procurement remains one of the region's largest economic sectors. According to estimates from the World Bank, procurement spending accounts for roughly 11.5% of GDP across the sub-region, totaling nearly $80 billion annually in public spending on goods, infrastructure, and services.

title: Kenya Railways Revives Gilgil-Nyahururu Branch Line, Signaling New Era for Agricultural Supply Chains
excerpt: Kenya Railways has resumed operations on the Gilgil-Nyahururu branch line, a corridor dormant for 46 years, opening new possibilities for freight and agricultural logistics.
author: Staff Writer
body:
In a landmark moment for Kenya's rail freight sector, Kenya Railways has resumed operations on the Gilgil-Nyahururu branch line, a corridor that had been dormant for 46 years.

The revival was inaugurated with the transportation of 396 tonnes of Government-subsidized fertilizer from Athi River Railway Station to National Cereals and Produce Board (NCPB) depots in Ol Kalou and Nyahururu, using 11 Metre Gauge Railway (MGR) wagons.

The movement marks the first cargo train to operate on this corridor since its closure, underscoring the strategic role rail transport can play in strengthening agricultural and commodity supply chains across the region.

The revival of the line has already attracted considerable interest from key industry stakeholders. OCP Kenya has expressed interest in fertilizer transportation, while Bamburi Cement PLC is eyeing the corridor for cement distribution.

New Kenya Cooperative Creameries (New KCC) sees potential for dairy logistics, and Autoports Freight Terminals Ltd. is exploring broader freight and logistics opportunities.

Additional sectors under consideration include construction materials such as sand and building stones, as well as livestock and related agricultural products.

This growing commercial interest signals that the Gilgil-Nyahururu corridor could evolve into a critical freight artery for central Kenya, opening new possibilities for businesses seeking cost-effective and reliable alternatives to road transport.

This development aligns with Kenya's broader Vision 2030 agenda, which positions railway transport as a key enabler of both micro and macroeconomic growth.
